notes: All foods — calories, fat, sat_fat, protein, fiber: Primary source for all nutritional values, reported per gram.

notes: Beef — nutrition verification: Confirmed nutritional values for 80% lean raw ground beef against USDA data.

title: ScienceDirect — Almond Water Footprint Study
notes: Almonds — water_per_kg: Reports 10,240 L/kg average for California commercial orchards.
Almonds — water_per_kg averaging: Averaged with Mekonnen & Hoekstra (source 30) value of 13,080 L/kg to produce final value of 11,660 L/kg.

title: Our World in Data — Almond Yields
notes: Almonds — yield_kg_ha: Verified ~3,000 kg/ha for California commercial orchards.

title: Our World in Data — Wheat Yields
notes: Wheat — yield_kg_ha: Source for global average of 2,067 kg/ha.

title: Our World in Data — Soybean Yields
notes: Soy — yield_kg_ha: Source for global average of 2,800 kg/ha.

title: Our World in Data — Corn (Maize) Yields
notes: Corn — yield_kg_ha: Source for world average of ~5,600 kg/ha.

title: USDA ERS — Feed Grains Sector at a Glance
notes: Corn — pesticide_kg_ha and fertilizer_kg_ha: Used for US corn agricultural statistics including pesticide and fertilizer application rates.

title: UC Davis — Alfalfa for Livestock
notes: Alfalfa — yield_kg_ha: Source for typical yield and agronomic characteristics.
Alfalfa — water_per_kg: Source for water use as livestock feed.
Alfalfa — human_food flag: Confirmed primarily a livestock feed crop (human_food = 0).

title: FAO — Alfalfa Crop Information
notes: Alfalfa — production statistics: FAO reference for alfalfa yield, water use, and agricultural characteristics.

title: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS)
notes: Grass hay — yield_kg_ha: Source for US hay yield and acreage data.
Sorghum — yield_kg_ha: Source for US sorghum acreage and yield data.

title: AgMRC — Sorghum
notes: Sorghum — yield_kg_ha: Agricultural Marketing Resource Center profile for sorghum yield and production statistics.

title: Wikipedia — List of Animals by Number of Neurons
notes: Beef cattle — neuron_count: ~3 billion neurons, based on Herculano-Houzel isotropic fractionator methodology.
Broiler chicken and layer hen — neuron_count: ~221 million neurons in domestic chicken.
Pig — neuron_count: ~2.23 billion neurons.
Tuna — neuron_count: ~30 million neurons.

title: UNL BeefWatch — Pounds of Meat from a Beef Animal
notes: Beef cattle — weight_kg: Source for typical slaughter weight.
Beef cattle — dressing percentage: 63%, used to convert live-weight FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.

title: Frontiers in Neuroanatomy — Chicken Neuron Count
notes: Chicken — neuron_count: Peer-reviewed source confirming 221 million neurons in domestic chicken (Red Junglefowl).

title: UMaine Extension — Understanding Poultry Yields
notes: Broiler chicken — dressing percentage: 72–75%, used to convert live-weight FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.

title: Feedlot Magazine — What's In A Feedlot Diet
notes: Beef cattle — feed composition: Source for feedlot finishing ration breakdown including corn, soy, alfalfa, hay, and sorghum percentages.

title: Wikipedia — Feed Conversion Ratio
notes: Beef cattle — FCR: ~6.0 kg feed per kg live weight gain for feedlot cattle.
Broiler chicken — FCR: ~1.6 kg feed per kg live weight gain for modern commercial broilers.
General: Reference for FCR definitions and species-level values.

title: Penn State Extension — Beef Carcass Yields and Losses
notes: Beef cattle — dressing percentage: 63%, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed beef.

title: Farm Progress — Alfalfa in Finishing Rations
notes: Beef cattle — feed composition: Confirmed alfalfa inclusion at ~10–12% of feedlot finishing ration.

title: Sorghum Checkoff — Beef Feeding Guide
notes: Beef cattle — feed composition: Source for sorghum inclusion rate (~5%) in beef cattle finishing diets.

title: Feed Strategy — Traditional US Broiler Feed Formulations
notes: Broiler chicken — feed composition: Corn 55–65%, soy 20–28%, sorghum 3–6%, varying by growth stage.

title: PMC — Atypical Feed Ingredients for Broiler Chickens
notes: Broiler chicken — feed composition: Peer-reviewed confirmation of typical sorghum inclusion rates in commercial broiler diets.

title: Our World in Data — Tomato Yields
notes: Tomato — yield_kg_ha: Global average of 38,000 kg/ha based on FAO data.

title: Our World in Data — Rice Yields
notes: Rice — yield_kg_ha: Global average paddy rice yield of 4,700 kg/ha based on FAO data.

title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra — Water Footprint of Crops (Report 47)
notes: General: Comprehensive peer-reviewed global crop water footprint database; primary source for most water_per_kg values.
Almonds — water_per_kg: 13,080 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect California study (source 7, 10,240 L/kg) → final 11,660 L/kg.
Walnuts — water_per_kg: 7,744 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 9,279 L/kg) → final 8,512 L/kg.
Pistachios — water_per_kg: 10,697 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 11,356 L/kg) → final 11,027 L/kg.
Hazelnuts — water_per_kg: 9,807 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 10,511 L/kg) → final 10,159 L/kg.
Peanuts — water_per_kg: 3,740 L/kg; averaged with secondary sources → final 3,537 L/kg.

title: Pig333 — Feed Conversion Ratio in Pigs
notes: Pig — FCR: ~2.8 kg feed per kg live weight gain.
Pig — dressing percentage: ~73%, used to calculate kg feed per kg dressed pork.

title: Seafish — Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile
notes: Farmed salmon — FCR: ~1.15 kg feed per kg live weight gain.
Farmed salmon — dressing percentage: ~90%, used to calculate kg feed per kg dressed fillet.
Farmed salmon — feed composition: Soy ~19%, wheat ~10% of diet by weight.

Walnuts — water_per_kg: 9,279 L/kg; averaged with Mekonnen & Hoekstra (source 30, 7,744 L/kg) → final 8,512 L/kg.
Pistachios — water_per_kg: 11,356 L/kg; averaged with Mekonnen & Hoekstra (source 30, 10,697 L/kg) → final 11,027 L/kg.

notes: Walnuts — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Cashews — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.
Pistachios — yield_kg_ha: Industry yield data.

title: Champrix — Feed Conversion Ratio in Layer Hens
notes: Layer hen — FCR (productive period only): ~2.2 kg feed per kg eggs during the laying phase.
Layer hen — FCR note: This is the laying-phase baseline only; see source 38 for the full lifetime calculation that also includes chick and pullet phases.

title: Alberta Milk — Feed Required per Litre of Milk
notes: Dairy cow — feed ratio (productive period only): ~1.35 kg feed per kg milk during active lactation.
Dairy cow — feed note: This is the lactation-phase baseline only; see source 39 for the full lifetime calculation that also includes dry periods and heifer rearing.

title: The Poultry Site — Layer Hen Feed and Production Over Lifetime
notes: Layer hen — lifetime feed: ~2 kg chick phase + ~9 kg pullet rearing + ~58 kg laying phase = ~69 kg total feed per bird.
Layer hen — lifetime egg output: ~300 eggs ≈ 17.1 kg eggs per bird over productive life.
Layer hen — lifetime FCR: 69 ÷ 17.1 ≈ 4.04 kg feed per kg eggs.
Layer hen — feed split applied: 60% corn / 20% soy → corn 2.42 kg, soy 0.81 kg per kg eggs.

title: Dairy Global — Feed Efficiency in Dairy Cows
notes: Dairy cow — lifetime feed breakdown: ~36,450 kg lactation feed (1.35 kg/kg milk × 27,000 kg milk) + ~2,700 kg dry-period feed (3 periods × 60 days × 15 kg/day) + ~5,840 kg heifer rearing (730 days × 8 kg DM/day) = ~44,990 kg total.
Dairy cow — lifetime milk output: ~27,000 kg per cow over productive life.
Dairy cow — lifetime FCR: 44,990 ÷ 27,000 ≈ 1.67 kg feed per kg milk.
Dairy cow — feed split applied: 55% corn / 25% alfalfa / 10% soy / 7% grass hay → corn 0.92, alfalfa 0.42, soy 0.17, hay 0.12 kg per kg milk.

title: EA Forum — Neuron Count Measures May Underweight Suffering in Farmed Fish
notes: Atlantic salmon — neuron_count: value used is 130,000,000 (130 million), derived as follows.

The commonly cited figure of 10 million neurons for salmon originates from an empirical count of a 0.75g zebrafish. Applying this to a ~3,000g Atlantic salmon is biologically indefensible — the figure does not scale with body or brain size.

Bruns & Davies (2022) ran a Monte Carlo simulation estimating 8–64 million neurons for salmon-sized fish, but this range was derived from data on fish under 4g and extrapolated across a >750× weight gap, so the uncertainty is substantial.

A scaling-adjusted estimate: applying Invincible Wellbeing's vertebrate curve-fitting formula to a 3,000g salmon yields ~1.9 billion neurons, but fish brains are roughly 1/15th the relative size of mammals and birds at equivalent body weight. Dividing by 15 gives ~130 million, which is the value used here.

All estimates carry significant uncertainty. The 130M figure is the most biologically grounded available estimate given the absence of direct neurological counts for Atlantic salmon at commercial harvest size.

title: Wikipedia — Domestic Turkey
notes: Turkey — weight_kg: Commercial tom turkeys at slaughter typically weigh 11–13 kg live weight; hens 6–8 kg. Value of 10 kg used as a weighted average reflecting the commercial flock mix.

Turkey — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for turkeys is available in the literature. As a closely related galliform to the domestic chicken, an estimate of ~262 million neurons is applied, slightly above the 221 million reported for chickens (Herculano-Houzel methodology), consistent with turkey brain mass being moderately larger than chicken brain mass at commercial slaughter size.

title: The Poultry Site — Turkey Production and Feed Conversion
notes: Turkey — FCR: Commercial turkeys achieve ~2.5–2.7 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 2.6 used.
Turkey — dressing percentage: ~80% for commercial toms, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.
Turkey — feed composition: Similar to broiler chicken — corn 55–60%, soybean meal 20–25%, wheat 8–12% of diet by weight.
Turkey — feed split applied: corn 60% × 2.6 FCR / 0.80 dressing = 1.95 kg corn/kg meat; soy 22% = 0.71 kg; wheat 10% = 0.33 kg per kg turkey meat.

title: PMC — Nervous Systems of Decapod Crustaceans
notes: Shrimp — neuron_count: Decapod crustaceans (including penaeoid shrimp) have been estimated at approximately 100,000–1,000,000 neurons in the central nervous system, with most commercial penaeid shrimp species falling near the lower end (~100,000–500,000). A value of 1,000,000 is used here as a conservative upper bound for commercial-sized shrimp (~15–20g), consistent with estimates for small decapods in the peer-reviewed literature.

title: WWF — Bycatch Overview
notes: Shrimp trawling — bycatch_amount: Gulf of Mexico and Southeast US shrimp trawl fisheries have historically reported bycatch-to-shrimp ratios of 3–10 kg discarded finfish per kg shrimp landed, with a commonly cited average of ~5.7 kg bycatch per kg shrimp.

Shrimp trawling — bycatch composition: Bycatch consists predominantly of small demersal and pelagic finfish, sea turtles (incidental), and invertebrates. Finfish bycatch is represented here using sardines (animal_id=10) as a proxy for small pelagic and demersal finfish.

Shrimp — wild vs. farmed: Approximately 55% of global shrimp production is wild-caught (predominantly trawl). The bycatch values apply to the wild-caught fraction. Farmed shrimp contribute no trawl bycatch but have separate feed and environmental impacts.

title: Wikipedia — Sardine
notes: Sardines — weight_kg: Commercial sardines (Sardina pilchardus, Sardinops sagax) at harvest typically weigh 50–150g; a value of 0.1 kg (100g) is used as representative.

Sardines — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for sardines exists in the peer-reviewed literature. As small teleost fish with brain structure comparable to other clupeoids, an estimate of ~10 million neurons is applied, consistent with figures used for small pelagic fish of similar body size (e.g., reference values for small fish in the range of 5–30 million neurons). This carries significant uncertainty.

title: FAO — The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture 2018
notes: Sardines — bycatch: Sardines are harvested primarily by purse seine, which is a highly selective method with bycatch rates generally below 5–10% of total catch weight, substantially lower than demersal trawl fisheries.

Sardines — bycatch_amount: A value of 0.1 kg bycatch per kg sardines landed is used, reflecting the low but non-zero incidental catch typical of small pelagic purse seine operations (primarily other small schooling fish and occasional juvenile finfish).

Sardines — bycatch_animal_id: Bycatch represented using tuna (animal_id=5) as a proxy for larger incidental finfish catch in pelagic fisheries, though actual bycatch species composition varies by region and season.

title: Wikipedia — Sheep
notes: Lamb — weight_kg: Commercial lambs are typically slaughtered at 40–55 kg live weight; a value of 45 kg is used.

Lamb — neuron_count: Sheep have been estimated at approximately 1.2 billion neurons based on isotropic fractionator data for ovine brain (Herculano-Houzel methodology). Sheep brain mass (~140g) is substantially smaller than cattle (~500g), consistent with the proportionally lower neuron count relative to beef cattle (~3 billion).

title: MLA — Sheep Feed and Nutrition
notes: Lamb — FCR: Commercial lambs on feedlot finishing achieve ~4.5–5.5 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 5.0 used for feedlot lamb.
Lamb — dressing percentage: ~50% for commercial lamb, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.
Lamb — feed composition: Finishing rations typically include corn 40–50%, soy 10–15%, grass hay 20–30%, alfalfa 10–20% by weight.
Lamb — feed split applied: corn 45% × 5.0 FCR / 0.50 dressing = 4.5 kg corn/kg meat; soy 12% = 1.2 kg; hay 28% = 2.8 kg; alfalfa 15% = 1.5 kg per kg lamb meat.

title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
notes: Farmed shrimp — feed composition: Commercial shrimp feeds typically contain soybean meal 25–35% and wheat 15–25% as primary plant-based ingredients; fishmeal provides 10–25% (animal origin, not tracked here).
Farmed shrimp — FCR: Modern semi-intensive and intensive penaeid shrimp aquaculture achieves FCR of ~1.3–1.6 kg feed per kg live shrimp; value of 1.5 used.
Farmed shrimp — dressing percentage: ~67% (heads-on whole shrimp converted to headless); plant feed values computed per kg headless shrimp.
Farmed shrimp — feed split applied: soy 30% × 1.5 / 0.67 = 0.67 kg soy/kg shrimp; wheat 20% × 1.5 / 0.67 = 0.45 kg wheat/kg shrimp. Applies to farmed fraction (~45% of global supply) only.

title: Fantke et al. (2014) — Ecotoxicological Characterization of Pesticides in USEtox (Science of the Total Environment)
notes: Pesticides — PAF (freshwater): Peer-reviewed LCA characterization factors for pesticide ecotoxicity in freshwater ecosystems using the USEtox model. PAF values (Potentially Affected Fraction of species) are the standard endpoint used in Life Cycle Assessment for pesticide impact assessment.
Glyphosate PAF: 0.00034 (freshwater, HC50 = 1.0E+05 mg/L range; very low aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
Atrazine PAF: 0.27 (freshwater, HC50 ~2 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity to algae and aquatic plants). Confidence 5.
Chlorpyrifos PAF: 0.48 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.001 µg/L; very high aquatic toxicity, especially to invertebrates). Confidence 5.
Malathion PAF: 0.22 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.01 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
Permethrin PAF: 0.56 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.0003 µg/L; very high aquatic toxicity to invertebrates and fish). Confidence 5.
Cypermethrin PAF: 0.61 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.00015 µg/L; extremely high aquatic toxicity). Confidence 5.
Imidacloprid PAF: 0.15 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.05 µg/L; high toxicity to aquatic invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Thiamethoxam PAF: 0.08 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.5 µg/L; moderate aquatic toxicity). Confidence 3.
Captan PAF: 0.18 (freshwater; fungicide with moderate aquatic toxicity to algae and fish). Confidence 3.
Mancozeb PAF: 0.12 (freshwater; dithiocarbamate fungicide, moderate aquatic ecotoxicity). Confidence 3.
Metolachlor PAF: 0.09 (freshwater, HC50 ~10 µg/L; low-moderate aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
2,4-D PAF: 0.05 (freshwater, HC50 ~100 µg/L; low aquatic toxicity). Confidence 4.
Pendimethalin PAF: 0.31 (freshwater, HC50 ~1 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity, especially to algae). Confidence 4.
Propiconazole PAF: 0.14 (freshwater; azole fungicide, moderate aquatic toxicity). Confidence 3.
Diazinon PAF: 0.39 (freshwater, HC50 ~0.01 µg/L; high aquatic toxicity to invertebrates). Confidence 4.
Paraquat PAF: 0.33 (freshwater; bipyridinium herbicide, high aquatic toxicity especially to algae). Confidence 3.
Carbaryl PAF: 0.26 (freshwater; carbamate insecticide, HC50 ~1 µg/L). Confidence 4.

title: Knauer et al. (2017) — ReCiPe Pesticide Characterization Factors (via EPA ecotoxicology database)
notes: Pesticides — PAF (freshwater): Supporting source for ReCiPe midpoint characterization factors for freshwater ecotoxicity of pesticides. Used as secondary confirmation for PAF values in source 50 where values were available.
Note: ReCiPe and USEtox frameworks yield broadly consistent PAF rankings, though absolute values differ slightly due to species sensitivity distribution assumptions.

title: USDA NASS — Agricultural Chemical Use Survey
notes: Pesticides — kg_ha per crop: Primary source for per-crop, per-pesticide application rates in the United States. USDA NASS conducts biennial surveys of pesticide use by crop and state. Values used are US national averages from the most recent survey cycles available (2018–2022 survey data).
Corn — atrazine: 1.12 kg/ha; glyphosate: 0.91 kg/ha; metolachlor: 0.68 kg/ha; pendimethalin: 0.05 kg/ha (minor use).
Soy — glyphosate: 0.84 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.03 kg/ha (minor); metolachlor: 0.14 kg/ha.
Wheat — 2,4-D: 0.38 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.06 kg/ha; propiconazole: 0.11 kg/ha; malathion: 0.04 kg/ha.
Cotton (reference crop, not in dataset): glyphosate: 0.95 kg/ha; pendimethalin: 0.47 kg/ha.
Potato — chlorpyrifos: 0.34 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.15 kg/ha; mancozeb: 1.2 kg/ha; metolachlor: 0.25 kg/ha.
Rice — carbaryl: 0.12 kg/ha; 2,4-D: 0.18 kg/ha; propiconazole: 0.05 kg/ha.
Sorghum — atrazine: 0.5 kg/ha; malathion: 0.06 kg/ha.
Peanuts — chlorpyrifos: 0.15 kg/ha; mancozeb: 0.4 kg/ha; pendimethalin: 0.35 kg/ha.
Oats — 2,4-D: 0.25 kg/ha; malathion: 0.03 kg/ha.
Lentils — pendimethalin: 0.22 kg/ha; malathion: 0.04 kg/ha.
Apple — captan: 1.2 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.12 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.5 kg/ha; malathion: 0.08 kg/ha; mancozeb: 0.3 kg/ha.
Alfalfa — malathion: 0.3 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.4 kg/ha; diazinon: 0.15 kg/ha.
Grass hay — 2,4-D: 0.25 kg/ha.
Tomato — metolachlor: 0.15 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.08 kg/ha; mancozeb: 0.5 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.12 kg/ha.
Broccoli — chlorpyrifos: 0.2 kg/ha; permethrin: 0.1 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.06 kg/ha; captan: 0.1 kg/ha.
Lettuce — imidacloprid: 0.05 kg/ha; chlorpyrifos: 0.08 kg/ha; permethrin: 0.07 kg/ha.
Cucumber — chlorpyrifos: 0.05 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.04 kg/ha; captan: 0.08 kg/ha.
Banana — chlorpyrifos: 0.5 kg/ha; paraquat: 1.5 kg/ha; mancozeb: 1.8 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.15 kg/ha.
Almonds — glyphosate: 1.5 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.12 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.8 kg/ha; captan: 0.5 kg/ha.
Walnuts — carbaryl: 0.6 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.1 kg/ha; glyphosate: 0.3 kg/ha.
Pistachios — imidacloprid: 0.08 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.3 kg/ha; glyphosate: 0.3 kg/ha.
Pecans — carbaryl: 0.7 kg/ha; malathion: 0.3 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.08 kg/ha.
Hazelnuts — carbaryl: 0.3 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.06 kg/ha. Limited NASS data; values drawn from UC extension.
Macadamia — glyphosate: 0.4 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.1 kg/ha; carbaryl: 0.3 kg/ha. Limited NASS data; Hawaii extension sources used.
Brazil nuts — pesticide use minimal; essentially wild-harvested. Values near zero.
Cashews — mancozeb: 0.2 kg/ha; imidacloprid: 0.07 kg/ha. Limited NASS data; tropical extension sources used.

Pine nuts — essentially wild-harvested or semi-managed from pinyon and stone pine forests; no reliable crop-specific pesticide survey data exists. The 0.5 kg/ha aggregate in plants.json reflects incidental forestry herbicide use (primarily glyphosate for site/understory management) extrapolated from general forestry literature. This attribution to glyphosate specifically is speculative — actual compound breakdown is unknown. Confidence 1.

title: Tang et al. (2019) — Global pesticide use and aquatic ecotoxicity (Chemosphere)
notes: Pesticides — kg_ha verification: Secondary peer-reviewed source for global pesticide application rates per crop type, used to cross-check USDA NASS values (source 52) for crops where US data may not reflect global averages.

Banana — mancozeb and chlorpyrifos: application rates in tropical banana production are substantially higher than temperate crops; 1.8 kg/ha mancozeb and 0.5 kg/ha chlorpyrifos consistent with published literature for Latin American and SE Asian production.
Rice — lower pesticide use overall compared to other staple crops globally; values consistent with FAO and NASS data.
General: supports use of USDA NASS as primary source for crops where US production is representative.

title: FAO/BfR — Tables on Weight Yield of Food and Retention Factors
notes: Animals — yield_fraction: Live weight to edible output conversion factors used to normalize feed conversion ratios to per-kg-food-eaten basis.
Beef: 0.43 (dressing ~62% of live weight × retail cut yield ~70% of carcass). Confidence 3.
Chicken: 0.75 (whole live bird to dressed edible, standard broiler). Confidence 4.
Pork: 0.70 (live to retail edible cuts). Confidence 4.
Salmon: 0.65 (whole fish to fillet). Confidence 3.
Tuna: 0.60 (whole fish to edible loin). Confidence 2.
Egg: 1.0 (feed conversion already expressed per kg egg output). Confidence 5.
Milk: 1.0 (feed conversion already expressed per kg milk output). Confidence 5.
Turkey: 0.80 (live to dressed edible). Confidence 4.
Shrimp: 0.60 (whole to peeled edible). Confidence 3.
Sardines: 0.75 (whole to edible flesh). Confidence 3.
Lamb: 0.45 (live to retail edible, similar structure to beef but lower yield). Confidence 3.
